Four additional agents (expected general availability, May 2026):[8]

The Career and Talent Development Agent automates succession planning and helps managers identify future leaders. It reasons over existing succession plans, prompts managers for updates when plans go stale, recommends successors based on skills and experience data, and generates personalized development plans. The operative word is "reasons", this is not a report. It is an agent that evaluates the quality of your succession pipeline and acts when it finds gaps.

The HR Service Agent functions as a first line of response for employee questions about company policies, benefits, and processes. Before routing a question to a human, it searches company policy documents and knowledge bases. If it cannot resolve the query, If it cannot resolve the query, it raises a ticket in SAP SuccessFactors Enterprise Service Management.

The People Intelligence Agent connects workforce analytics in SAP Business Data Cloud with Joule's conversational interface. HR leaders can ask questions in natural language, about attrition risk, skills distribution, or capability gaps, and receive visual analytics with recommendations, not just data tables.

The Payroll Agent addresses one of the most common sources of employee frustration: understanding pay. Building on SAP's existing Explain Pay feature in Joule, which integrates data from SuccessFactors time tracking and reportedly reduces pay-related resolution time by up to 50%, the Payroll Agent extends this capability to both employees and payroll administrators, providing natural-language explanations for pay changes connected to specific time entries like overtime, absences, or shift differentials.[9]

At SAP Connect 2025, SAP announced more than a dozen new Joule Agents spanning finance, procurement, supply chain, and HR, part of a broader expansion that brought the total to more than 40 agents across SAP's portfolio.[10] The pattern across all of them is consistent: each agent is domain-specific, operates within enterprise security boundaries, and is designed to reason and act, not just respond.

Additionally, SAP has introduced a workforce knowledge network that integrates third-party content from industry experts, including G-P (Globalization Partners) and The Josh Bersin Company, directly into Joule, enabling agent-to-agent interaction that grounds recommendations in external research and best practices.[11]

Joule Studio: building custom agents

Ready-made agents address common workflows. But every organization has processes that are unique, and this is where Joule Studio enters the picture.

Joule Studio, a capability within SAP Build, reached general availability for skill building in July 2025 and for agent building in January 2026.[12] It allows organizations to create custom Joule agents and skills using a low-code interface, grounded in SAP's Knowledge Graph and business data.

Two capabilities make Joule Studio strategically significant.

First, Model Context Protocol (MCP) support allows custom agents to connect with external tools and non-SAP systems through an open standard.[13] This is not a proprietary integration framework. An HR agent built in Joule Studio can pull compensation benchmarking data from a third-party system and combine it with internal SuccessFactors data to make a recommendation, breaking down the data silos that consistently impede AI value.

Second, multi-agent orchestration allows agents to function as tools for one another. Joule can invoke a recruiting agent and a workforce planning agent in sequence to solve a problem that spans both domains, identifying a skills gap, finding internal candidates, and flagging external hiring needs in a single workflow.[14] This coordination is what distinguishes an agent ecosystem from a collection of disconnected tools.

Early adopters are building production-grade agents quickly. At SAP's Hack2Build event in January 2026, partner teams, including Capgemini, which described Joule Studio as enabling connectivity across SAP and non-SAP environments in hybrid landscapes, created functional custom agents in under seven days.[15]

The governance gap

The capability is moving fast. The governance is not.

The data on this point is unambiguous. While 74% of companies expect to use agentic AI within two years, only 21% have a mature model for governance of autonomous agents.[1] The risks that concern organizations most all relate to governance: data privacy and security (73%), legal and regulatory compliance (50%), and governance capabilities and oversight (46%).[1]

For HR, the governance challenge is acute. AI agents in HR make decisions that affect people's careers, compensation, development opportunities, and access to roles. An agent that recommends successors shapes who gets developed. An agent that answers policy questions defines how policy is interpreted. An agent that explains pay determines whether employees trust their employer.

These are not edge cases. They are the core of what HR does.

A minimum viable governance model for agentic AI in HR should address several critical dimensions:

Permissions inheritance. Role-based permissions must extend cleanly to agents, if a manager cannot see certain employee data, their agent cannot either. SAP has built this into Joule's architecture through principal propagation, meaning the user's identity is passed end-to-end so that any agent action runs within that user's security context.[5]

Human-in-the-loop checkpoints. High-stakes decisions, succession nominations, compensation recommendations, talent mobility, require defined approval points. Joule Studio enables human-in-the-loop by default.[12]

Auditability. Organizations must be able to explain why an agent made a specific recommendation. This is particularly important as regulatory frameworks like the EU AI Act impose transparency requirements on high-risk AI systems.

Bias monitoring. When agents influence talent decisions at scale, bias monitoring must be operationalized, not just discussed. This is especially critical for agents involved in succession planning, career development, and internal mobility recommendations.

Platform guardrails are necessary but not sufficient. The harder work is organizational: defining which decisions agents can make autonomously, which require human approval, and how to measure whether agents are making those decisions fairly.

The economic case

The investment case for agentic AI in HR is strengthening, but executives should approach the numbers with appropriate nuance.

SAP's Value of AI Report, conducted with Oxford Economics in 2025, found that organizations are seeing an average 16% ROI on AI investments (approximately $4.7 million), with expectations for that to nearly double to 31% within two years. Seventy-nine percent expect positive ROI in less than three years.[16]

However, Deloitte's research provides important counterweight. Most organizations achieve satisfactory ROI on AI use cases within two to four years, significantly longer than the seven-to-twelve–month payback period typically expected for technology investments.[17] And 65% of respondents in SAP's own study acknowledge they are unsure or do not believe AI is delivering its full potential, even as they report positive returns.[16]

The reconciliation is straightforward. AI delivers measurable productivity gains on narrow use cases, drafting, summarization, Q&A automation, relatively quickly. The transformative impact, redesigning processes, redeploying talent, making better strategic decisions, takes longer and requires organizational change that most companies are still building.

For HR leaders, the practical implication is a dual-speed strategy. Capture quick wins with copilot-level capabilities (Joule answering employee queries, drafting performance reviews, explaining pay) while investing in the data quality, governance, and change management required for agent-level capabilities (automated succession planning, skills-based talent recommendations, workforce analytics on demand).

What this means for the HR operating model

The deeper question is not whether to deploy Joule agents, but what happens to HR when agents handle an increasing share of operational work.

Deloitte and ServiceNow's 2026 enterprise research describes a future where employees increasingly serve as "orchestrators and supervisors of AI agents rather than direct executors of tasks."[18] For HR, this implies a structural shift in how the function allocates its capacity.

Consider the HR Service Center. Today, a significant portion of HR service delivery involves answering repetitive questions, leave policies, benefits eligibility, payroll explanations, onboarding logistics. If an HR Service Agent handles a majority of these queries autonomously, the freed capacity creates space for HR professionals to focus on strategic workforce planning, manager coaching, organizational design, and the contextual employee support that no agent can replicate.

SAP itself is modeling this internally. Its People & Culture Lounge concept, announced in late 2025, provides all 110,000 SAP employees access to one-on-one conversations with HR experts. The rationale is explicit: because technologies like Joule resolve standard inquiries efficiently, personal conversation in complex situations becomes more important, not less.[19]

The pattern is instructive: agents handle volume. Humans handle judgment. The organizations that get the division of labor right will have HR functions that are both more efficient and more human, not one at the expense of the other.

A practical path forward

For SuccessFactors customers evaluating Joule's agentic capabilities, the path forward requires sequencing technology, data, and governance in the right order.

Start with data readiness, not agent deployment. Every agent is only as good as the data it reasons over. If your job architecture is inconsistent, your skills taxonomy is unmaintained, or your performance data is sparse, agents will produce unreliable recommendations, and users will lose trust before the technology gets a chance to prove itself. Clean your Talent Intelligence Hub data. Ensure Growth Portfolios are populated. Validate that role-based permissions are correctly configured.

Activate Joule and capture copilot-level value now. Joule's conversational capabilities, answering employee questions, drafting content, navigating SuccessFactors, are available today and deliver immediate productivity returns. Build user familiarity and organizational comfort with AI in HR workflows before introducing agents that act autonomously.

Deploy the Performance and Goals Agent as your first agent. It is generally available, scoped to a well-understood process, and delivers value that managers will notice immediately. Use it to establish governance patterns, human-in-the-loop checkpoints, feedback mechanisms, monitoring protocols, that you can extend to more complex agents later.

Build governance before you need it. Define which talent decisions require human approval. Establish auditability standards for agent recommendations. Create bias monitoring protocols for agents that influence succession, development, and career pathing. The May 2026 wave of agents will arrive whether you are ready or not.

Plan for Joule Studio when ready-made agents are not enough. If your organization has unique workflows, custom onboarding sequences, industry-specific compliance processes, bespoke talent review cycles, Joule Studio allows you to create custom agents grounded in your business data. But this is a Phase 2 capability. Get the fundamentals right first.

Where to start

The trajectory is unmistakable. Joule is evolving from a conversational interface into an agentic layer that will increasingly participate in, not just support, HR decision-making. The Performance and Goals Agent is the first tangible proof point. The Career and Talent Development, HR Service, People Intelligence, and Payroll agents arriving in 2026 will extend this pattern across the HR lifecycle.

But the technology is ahead of most organizations' readiness. The data is clear: agentic AI governance lags adoption by a wide margin. The organizations that invest now in data quality, permissions architecture, and governance frameworks will be positioned to capture value from agents as they mature. Those that wait for agents to force the issue will spend their time managing risk instead of creating value.

Joule is not a chatbot. Increasingly, it is not even a copilot. It is becoming a new category of HR participant, one that reasons, acts, and learns within the same systems your people use every day. The question for HR leaders is not whether to engage with this shift. It is whether they will shape it, or be shaped by it.

What is your organization's readiness for agentic AI in HR? I'd welcome a conversation, particularly from those who have deployed the Performance and Goals Agent or are planning for the May 2026 wave.

References

 

  1. Deloitte, State of AI in the Enterprise, 2026 Edition
  2. SAP, SAP Announces New Generative AI Assistant Joule
  3. Deloitte, Tech Trends 2026
  4. Deloitte, Agentic AI Strategy, Tech Trends 2026
  5. SAP, Joule Artificial Intelligence Assistant
  6. SAP, What's New with SAP Business AI, Joule, and Joule Agents in the SAP SuccessFactors 2H 2025 Release
  7. SAP, Innovation Guide H2 2025
  8. SAP, New Joule Agents and Embedded Intelligence Supercharge Business Returns
  9. SAP, Agent Builder in Joule Studio Is Now Generally Available
  10. SAP, Build Business-Grounded AI Agents, Faster
  11. SAP, Unprecedented Possibilities: New SAP SuccessFactors Innovations
  12. SAP, Joule Studio
  13. SAP, Build Business-Grounded AI Agents, Faster
  14. SAP, Partner-Driven Innovation Takes Off with Agent Builder in Joule Studio
  15. SAP and Oxford Economics, The Value of AI Report
  16. Deloitte, AI ROI: The Paradox of Rising Investment and Elusive Returns
  17. Deloitte and ServiceNow, The AI-Fueled Enterprise of 2026
  18. SAP, More Than Just Technology: Why Personal HR Support Gains Importance in the Age of AI

entry.title: Troubleshooting Guide: "To Be Hired" Status Not Updating in SAP SuccessFactors Manage Positions
entry.content:
Troubleshooting by Scenario:

1. Issues During Import Processes

Scenario A: Job Information Import Not Updating "To Be Hired" Status

When this occurs:

  • Assign or unassign users via job information import

Root Causes & Solutions:

Cause 1: Follow-up processes not configured

  • Symptoms: Import completes successfully but "To Be Hired" status remains unchanged
  • Solution: Configure HrisSLEventPublishConfig in Manage Data
    1. Navigate to Manage Data
    2. Create "Centralized Services HRIS Event Publishing Configuration" object
    3. If object not found: Configure Object Definitions → Search for "Centralized Services HRIS Event Publishing Configuration" → Make Corrections → Add label → Save
    4. Grant user permissions under Miscellaneous Permissions

Cause 2: Position Management Settings not enabled

  • Symptoms: Imports work but "To Be Hired" status doesn’t adapt
  • Solution: Enable adaptation setting
    1. Go to Position Management Settings → Import tab
    2. Set "Adapt Position 'To Be Hired' Status After Imports" to YES

Scenario B: Termination Import Not Updating Status

When this occurs:

  • Importing termination records
  • API-based termination processing

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Expected Behavior: Termination imports don’t trigger "To Be Hired" adaptation
  • Reason: System only adapts when position changes occur, not employment status changes
  • Workaround: Manual position record update required after termination

2. Issues with UI Operations

Scenario C: MSS UI Changes Not Updating Position

When this occurs:

  • Using "Change Job and Compensation Info" in MSS
  • Making position changes through Edit button

Root Causes & Solutions:

Cause 1: Position previously assigned via Job Information History

  • Symptoms: MSS UI changes don’t trigger "To Be Hired" status updates
  • Solution: This is expected behavior - position won’t adapt if originally assigned through Job History
  • Workaround: Use Add New Employee or direct MSS UI for initial assignments

Cause 2: Missing FTE calculation rules

  • Symptoms: Position assignment completes but "To Be Hired" status doesn’t change
  • Solution: Configure FTE calculation business rules
    1. Create business rule using "Calculate Full-Time Equivalent (FTE)" scenario
    2. Set as onChange rule (on custom standard-hours field) OR Set as onSave rule (on jobInfo element)

Cause 3: Missing mandatory fields in position

  • Symptoms: Position change is successful but "To Be Hired" not updated
  • Solution: Complete required position fields
    1. Navigate to Manage Positions
    2. Find affected position → Make Correction
    3. Fill all mandatory fields

Scenario 😧 Job Information History Changes

When this occurs:

  • Making corrections in Job Information History portlet
  • Adding new records in job history

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Expected Behavior: Changes in Job Information History don’t update "To Be Hired"
  • Solution: Use proper channels for position updates:
    1. Add New Employee wizard for new hires
    2. MSS UI "Change Job and Compensation Info" or Edit button for existing employees

3. Issues with Onboarding and Recruiting Integration

Scenario E: API-Based Onboarding Not Updating Position

When this occurs:

  • Using APIs for onboarding processes

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Expected Behavior: Position Management doesn’t support follow-up processes for Onboarding API changes
  • Manual Solution: Update position records manually after API-based hiring

Scenario F: Crossboarding/Internal Hire Issues

When this occurs:

  • Initiating crossboarding via Recruiting
  • Internal transfers and promotions

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Cause: "Enable Additional Internal Hire Activities" setting disabled
  • Solution:
    1. Go to Admin Center → Onboarding General Settings
    2. Enable "Enable Additional Internal Hire Activities" option

4. Issues with Multiple Incumbents and FTE Management

Scenario G: Multiple Incumbent Positions

When this occurs:

  • Positions allowing multiple incumbents
  • FTE-based position management

Root Causes & Solutions:

Cause 1: positionControlled field not editable

  • Symptoms: FTE calculations incorrect despite correct assignments
  • Solution:
    1. Go to Configure Object Definitions → Position
    2. Set positionControlled field to "editable"

Cause 2: FTE setting configuration

  • Symptoms: Position not updating when FTE appears reached
  • Solution: Adjust Position Management Settings
    1. Navigate to Position Management Settings → General
    2. Change "Set or Reset 'To Be Hired' Status if an Incumbent’s 'FTE' is Changed" to "Always" (instead of "Only if Planned FTE Value is Reached")

Cause 3: Effective dating logic with future hires

  • Symptoms: Current FTE reached but status not updating due to future-dated assignments
  • Solution: Ensure employees assigned with appropriate effective dates that reflect current staffing needs

5. Issues with Position Reclassification

Scenario H: Position Reclassification Creating New Positions

When this occurs:

  • Using Event Reasons with "Position Reclassification" follow-up activity
  • Position updates result in new position creation

Root Causes & Solutions:

Cause 1: Search setting disabled

  • Solution:
    1. Go to Position Management Settings
    2. Set "Search for Position in Position Reclassification" to "Yes"

Cause 2: Field matching issues

  • Symptoms: System cannot find matching vacant position
  • Solution: Ensure vacant positions have same field values (Department, Division, Custom Fields) or include them in Job-to-Position sync rules

6. Issues with Future Dating and Transition Periods

Scenario I: Future-Dated Hires with transition period

When this occurs:

  • Processing candidates with future start dates
  • Managing transition periods between incumbents

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Expected Behavior: "To Be Hired" reflects effective date, not process completion date.  It is currently not possible to update the 'to be hired' status to NO, if a person is terminated in the future and the 'successor' gets assigned to the same position BEFORE the other incumbent is leaving.

To retrigger the "To Be Hired" status adaptation, navigate to Admin Alerts → Position Follow-up Processes Alerts and retrigger the process

If it still fails, you can raise a ticket to product support.

Reference:
Enabling To Be Hired Status Adaptation on the Position (Change from Employee Data Import) | SAP Help Portal
Adapt the To Be Hired Status on the Position (Change from UI) | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: Useful steps for admins when migrating from SuccessFactors Legacy People Profile to Full Profile
entry.content:
With Full Profile of the New People Profile on the horizon and being a mandatory change for 1H 2026, here are some practical/admin tips for your transition from Legacy People Profile (LPP) to Full Profile (FP)

1. Clean-up your LPP before running the FP migration job.

Remove any unnecessary, empty, or hidden sections, sub-sections, and blocks before starting the migration. This ensures that the migration job includes only the required portlets, resulting in a cleaner FP environment and eliminating the need for post-migration cleanup.

There are chances that admins may have created these unwanted sections for some testing purposes in the past but never permissioned. So, do a check before initiating the migration job.

2. Understand what is supported and not supported in the FP and how do you identify what migrated across to the FP and what left behind?

Some portlets of LPP such as mini-org chart, performance-potential matrix, functional experience etc. are not supported in FP. So, even when you run the migration job, they are not migrated over to the FP. Therefore, it is important to know what got migrated and not when you ran the job.

How do identify what migrated across to the FP in your system?

When you run the FP migration job from Company Systems & Logo settings page, extract the Download Status file from the Scheduled Job Manager. This gives a comprehensive list of what got migrated and what was left out. It helps you understand old blocks vs new cards in your system. Some screenshots below:

Scheduled Job Manager

Sample of log file

Note: If you have already converted to FP and have done some work on FP, re-running the Migration job would wipe off your FP work and make it a clean slate. So, do it with due diligence.

If you want to switch back to old profile for any testing, you can switch back by unticking the ‘Enable Full Profile for the Latest People Profile’ and revert to FP again by ticking it. This way, your FP work won’t erase.

 

3. Work on the FP Layout:

Once you get a clean transition, and only with wanted categories & cards, now, it is up to the customers to work on the layout. Obviously, you will have an option to rearrange categories and some cards across & within categories. Work on this in consultation with your business and make it suitable as per your business requirements.

Depending on your requirements, you may have to work on RBPs’ here and what you want to see on the cards. You may have to do some RBP tweaks and ordering of the fields so that the most useful information remains on top of the card without needing to dig into the card. (wear a confidentiality lens when doing this)

4. Understand the two-layer permissions:

While your original RBP drives the access to the fields, your category level permissions drives visibility to the categories. Using a RBP Permissions vs Roles Table Report, you can identify which roles has access to the sections/employee views in the LPP and mimic the permissions for Categories in the FP.

For example, from the above-mentioned table report, you identified that the Compensation section (Employee View) in the LPP is permissioned for X, Y, Z roles. Now, in the FP, you can provide the same roles (X, Y, Z) access to the Compensation category and this way you are ensuring that the relevant roles have access to the categories in the FP. (Once you start working on this, you will get a hang on this. If you need any help on the report, feel free to contact me and happy to help).

5. Use Configuration Transport Centre (CTC) to migrate your changes across SF instances. (This is truly a life saver)

Believe me, it is indeed a life saver!

Assuming you have started & developed all your work in a test instance before moving to production, using a CTC will save your time, effort and an efficient way to copy your changes across the instances.

Through CTC, your entire FP config will copy across the instances. The ordering of the categories, cards, category permissions, any text changes config will all copy across.

In my experience, the config in the General settings i.e., Header fields, enable latest Editing & History UI etc. of the FP did not copy across, but these are just couple of ticks, so that worries least for any admins/customers. You can manually tick them once your category level config is copied over.

6. Finally, some quick unit testing to make sure everything is done properly!

entry.title: List of Employee Central & Employee Central Payroll Guide Updates for the 1H 2026 Release
entry.content:
In SAP SuccessFactors User Assistance, we’ve been working on improving your information experience.

Latest People Profile

We have made more enhancements to the latest People Profile, including redesigned Editing UI and History UI. For more information, refer to Latest People Profile. 

Country/Region-Specific Dependent Configuration

We have added a new section to the Implementing Dependents guide to help you take advantage of the new country/region-specific dependent configuration feature. For more information, refer to Implementing Dependents.

Understanding Settings for Manage Business Configuration

We have added a new topic to the Setting Up Data Models and Managing Configuration guide to understand more details about the features you can enable in your system. We have added more information about dependencies between the settings as well as links to the related guides. For more information, refer to topic in the guide.

Release Center

The Release Center adds the What’s New Viewer content to the Admin Center. It automatically filters the What's New Viewer content based on your tenant's configuration and gives you tools to collaborate with other administrators in your company.

For more information, refer to the  Release Center Adds What's New to Admin Center | SAP Help Portal WNV topic and the What’s New, Now Relevant: Introducing Release Cent... - SAP Community blog.

Admin Page Revamp

We have revamped a few of the admin pages, for example, Company Settings, to meet SAP user interface standards. The pages have all the same settings and functions. For more information, refer to Administrator Pages Revamp for 1H 2026 | SAP Help Portal.

entry.title: SAP RPT-1 Meets Employee Attrition: A Hands-On Test with SuccessFactors Data
entry.content:
The Short Version

I wanted to see if SAP’s new tabular AI model, RPT-1, could predict which employees are likely to quit, using nothing but a CSV extracted from SuccessFactors fields. No model training. No data science team. Just upload and predict.

To keep things honest, I based the experiment on a real peer-reviewed study that used actual employee data and traditional ML. The result? RPT-1 nailed all 5 test predictions in under 400 milliseconds. But the full story is more interesting than the headline.

 

Why I Picked Attrition (and Not Another Sales Demo)

Credit where it's due: Hari Panakkal's recent SAP Community post, Experiment with SAP RPT-1: Predicting the Right Carrier in SAP TM Using the No-Code Playground, is a great walkthrough of RPT-1 on transportation data. It got me thinking: if RPT-1 works that well for carrier prediction in TM, what happens when you point it at an HR problem?

Most RPT-1 demos I've seen focus on sales forecasting or logistics. Those are solid use cases, but the one I care about is people analytics, and the single biggest question HR teams ask is: "Who's about to leave?"

Attrition is a natural fit for RPT-1 because:

  • The data is tabular and already sitting in SuccessFactors
  • The problem is binary classification (stays vs. leaves)
  • Most HR teams don't have ML engineers on staff

But I didn't want to just throw random HR data at it. I wanted to test RPT-1 against something with a known answer.

 

The Research Behind the Experiment

I grounded everything in this study:

Manafi Varkiani, S., Pattarin, F., Fabbri, T., & Fantoni, G. (2025). Predicting employee attrition and explaining its determinants. Expert Systems With Applications, 272, 126575.

Here’s what makes this paper worth paying attention to:

It used real data. Not overused Kaggle dataset, but actual records from an Italian financial company. 5,767 employees. 309 voluntary resignations (a 5.4% attrition rate, which is realistic and makes the problem genuinely hard).

They tested four algorithms head-to-head. Random Forest crushed everything: 99.9% AUC, 94.4% sensitivity. Logistic Regression and Decision Trees weren’t even close.

They found things that don’t match your intuition. This is the best part. Using SHAP analysis, they discovered that employees who received bonuses were more likely to leave (burnout from overperforming), and employees who got retention incentives also showed higher attrition. It turns out retention incentives are targeted at people already flagged as flight risks, so the incentive is a signal of existing risk, not a cause of it.

The ML pipeline was serious work. ROSE resampling for class imbalance, 10-fold cross-validation, hyperparameter tuning, and SHAP post-processing. This took significant data science effort.

So the question became: Can RPT-1 get comparable results with a single API call?

 

Mapping the Variables to SuccessFactors

The study used 14 employee attributes. I mapped each one to the actual OData entity and field in SuccessFactors, and validated every mapping against the live API:

 

Research Variable

Where It Lives in SF

Category

Gender

PerPersonal.gender

Personal

Age Class

Derived from PerPerson.dateOfBirth

Personal

Tenure Class

Derived from EmpEmployment.startDate

People

Department

EmpJob.department

Work Environment

Rank

EmpJob.payGrade → FOPayGrade (grouped)

Management

Contract Type

EmpJob.contractType

Management

Job Level

EmpJob.payGrade

Management

Talent Program

TalentPool membership

People

Bonus Received

EmpPayCompNonRecurring existence

Management

Hours (PT/FT)

EmpJob.standardHours

Management

Retention Incentive

Custom MDF or EmpPayCompRecurring

Management

Job Family

FOJobCode.jobFunction

Management

Log Salary

EmpPayCompRecurring.paycompvalue (log10)

Management

Log Premium

Performance bonus (log10)

Management

Picture 1: Ishikawa – Fishbone diagram

 

What Is RPT-1, Exactly?

SAP RPT-1 is a Relational Pretrained Transformer , a foundation model built for tabular data, not text. Announced at TechEd 2025, it comes in three flavors:

  • sap-rpt-1-small : Fast commercial model (low latency)
  • sap-rpt-1-large : Higher accuracy commercial model
  • sap-rpt-1-oss : Open-source on GitHub (Apache 2.0)

The key idea: you give it labeled rows as examples and rows with [PREDICT] placeholders as questions. It learns from the context: no training, no feature engineering, no resampling. Think of it like few-shot prompting in ChatGPT, but for spreadsheets.

 

Running the Experiment

Step 1: Build the Dataset

I created sf_employee_attrition.csv with 50 labeled employee records using the 14 features from the research, plus 5 query rows with [PREDICT] in the ATTRITION column. The labeled data reflects the patterns the study found: higher attrition among younger, shorter-tenure employees with lower compensation, and the counterintuitive bonus/retention effects.

 

Picture 2 - sf_employee_attrition.csv, 50 labeled + 5 query rows, 14 features mapped to SF fields

 

Step 2: The No-Code Route (Playground)

Easiest way to test. Go to rpt.cloud.sap/dashboard:

  1. Upload the CSV
  2. The interface detects the [PREDICT] cells
  3. Click Predict
  4. Done

No coding. Supports up to 2,073 rows and 50 columns.

 

Step 3: The API Route (for Pipelines)

This option allows full control and evaluation metrics:

"""
SAP RPT-1 Employee Attrition Prediction
========================================
This script demonstrates how to use SAP-RPT-1 (Relational Pretrained Transformer)
to predict employee attrition using structured HR data modeled after
SuccessFactors workforce analytics dimensions.

Dataset design informed by: Manafi Varkiani et al. (2025),
"Predicting employee attrition and explaining its determinants",
Expert Systems With Applications, 272, 126575.


Prerequisites:
  - For API approach: SAP RPT Playground account (https://rpt.cloud.sap)
  - For OSS approach: Python 3.11+, GPU with 24-80GB VRAM recommended

Author: Shakti Barath
"""

import json
import csv
import requests
import os
from pathlib import Path

# ============================================================
# CONFIGURATION
# ============================================================

# Option A: Load token from file (recommended)
# Create access_token.json with: {"token": "YOUR_TOKEN_HERE"}
# Get your token from: https://rpt.cloud.sap/docs (scroll to bottom)
TOKEN_FILE = "access_token.json"

# Option B: Set as environment variable
# export SAP_RPT_TOKEN="your_token_here"

RPT_API_URL = "https://rpt.cloud.sap/api/predict"
CSV_FILE = "sf_employee_attrition.csv"
TARGET_COLUMN = "ATTRITION"
PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER = "[PREDICT]"

def load_token():
    """Load API token from file or environment variable."""
    if os.path.exists(TOKEN_FILE):
        with open(TOKEN_FILE) as f:
            data = json.load(f)
            return data.get("token", "")
    return os.environ.get("SAP_RPT_TOKEN", "")

def load_csv_as_rows(filepath):
    """
    Load CSV and convert to RPT-1 row format.
    
    RPT-1 expects rows as a list of dicts. Rows with [PREDICT] in the 
    target column serve as query rows; all others are context rows
    (few-shot examples for in-context learning).
    """
    rows = []
    with open(filepath, newline="", encoding="utf-8") as f:
        reader = csv.DictReader(f)
        for row in reader:
            rows.append(dict(row))
    return rows

def build_payload(rows, target_column, index_column="EMP_ID"):
    """
    Build the RPT-1 API request payload.
    
    Structure:
    - rows: list of dicts (context + query rows)
    - index_column: unique identifier column
    - prediction_config: specifies target and task type
    
    Query rows contain [PREDICT] in the target column.
    Context rows contain actual labels — these are the few-shot examples.
    """
    payload = {
        "rows": rows,
        "index_column": index_column,
        "prediction_config": {
            "target_columns": [
                {
                    "name": target_column,
                    "placeholder_value": PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER,
                    "task_type": "classification"
                }
            ]
        }
    }
    return payload

def call_rpt1_api(payload, token):
    """Send prediction request to SAP RPT-1 Playground API."""
    headers = {
        "Authorization": f"Bearer {token}",
        "Content-Type": "application/json",
        "Accept": "application/json"
    }
    
    print("Sending prediction request to SAP RPT-1...")
    print(f"  Context rows: {sum(1 for r in payload['rows'] if r.get(TARGET_COLUMN) != PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER)}")
    print(f"  Query rows:   {sum(1 for r in payload['rows'] if r.get(TARGET_COLUMN) == PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER)}")
    
    response = requests.post(RPT_API_URL, headers=headers, json=payload, timeout=120)
    response.raise_for_status()
    return response.json()

def display_predictions(result, rows):
    """Display RPT-1 predictions alongside employee context."""
    predictions = result.get("prediction", {}).get("predictions", [])
    
    # Build lookup for employee context
    emp_lookup = {str(r["EMP_ID"]): r for r in rows}
    
    print("\n" + "=" * 80)
    print("SAP RPT-1 ATTRITION PREDICTIONS")
    print("=" * 80)
    
    for pred in predictions:
        emp_id = str(pred.get("id", ""))
        emp = emp_lookup.get(emp_id, {})
        attrition_pred = pred.get(TARGET_COLUMN, [{}])
        
        if isinstance(attrition_pred, list) and len(attrition_pred) > 0:
            predicted_value = attrition_pred[0].get("prediction", "N/A")
            confidence = attrition_pred[0].get("confidence")
        else:
            predicted_value = "N/A"
            confidence = None
        
        risk_indicator = "HIGH RISK" if predicted_value == "Yes" else "LOW RISK"
        
        print(f"\n  Employee {emp_id}: {emp.get('JOB_FAMILY', 'Unknown')}")
        print(f"  Department: {emp.get('DEPARTMENT', '-')} | "
              f"Age: {emp.get('AGE_CLASS', '-')} | "
              f"Tenure: {emp.get('TENURE_CLASS', '-')}")
        print(f"  Talent Program: {emp.get('TALENT_PROGRAM', '-')} | "
              f"Contract: {emp.get('CONTRACT_TYPE', '-')}")
        print(f"  Prediction: {predicted_value} ({risk_indicator})")
        if confidence is not None:
            print(f"  Confidence: {confidence:.2%}")
    
    print("\n" + "=" * 80)

def analyze_context_patterns(rows):
    """
    Analyze attrition patterns in context data.
    Mirrors the descriptive statistics approach from the research paper
    (Manafi Varkiani et al., 2025, Table 6).
    """
    context_rows = [r for r in rows if r.get(TARGET_COLUMN) != PREDICT_PLACEHOLDER]
    
    print("\n" + "-" * 60)
    print("CONTEXT DATA ANALYSIS (mirrors research methodology)")
    print("-" * 60)
    
    # Attrition by age class
    age_stats = {}
    for r in context_rows:
        age = r["AGE_CLASS"]
        left = r[TARGET_COLUMN] == "Yes"
        if age not in age_stats:
            age_stats[age] = {"total": 0, "left": 0}
        age_stats[age]["total"] += 1
        age_stats[age]["left"] += int(left)
    
    print("\nAttrition Rate by Age Class:")
    for age in sorted(age_stats.keys()):
        s = age_stats[age]
        rate = (s["left"] / s["total"] * 100) if s["total"] > 0 else 0
        print(f"  {age:12s}: {rate:5.1f}%  ({s['left']}/{s['total']})")
    
    # Attrition by tenure class
    tenure_stats = {}
    for r in context_rows:
        tenure = r["TENURE_CLASS"]
        left = r[TARGET_COLUMN] == "Yes"
        if tenure not in tenure_stats:
            tenure_stats[tenure] = {"total": 0, "left": 0}
        tenure_stats[tenure]["total"] += 1
        tenure_stats[tenure]["left"] += int(left)
    
    print("\nAttrition Rate by Tenure Class:")
    for tenure in sorted(tenure_stats.keys()):
        s = tenure_stats[tenure]
        rate = (s["left"] / s["total"] * 100) if s["total"] > 0 else 0
        print(f"  {tenure:12s}: {rate:5.1f}%  ({s['left']}/{s['total']})")
    
    # Key insight from the research
    print("\n  Key Insight (Manafi Varkiani et al., 2025):")
    print("  Younger employees and those with shorter tenure show")
    print("  significantly higher attrition rates. The research found")
    print("  15.9% attrition for age 18-25 vs 1.4% for age 50-65.")

# ============================================================
# MAIN EXECUTION
# ============================================================

def main():
    print("=" * 80)
    print("SAP RPT-1: Employee Attrition Prediction Experiment")
    print("Grounded in: Manafi Varkiani et al. (2025), ESWA 272")
    print("=" * 80)
    
    # Load data
    if not Path(CSV_FILE).exists():
        print(f"ERROR: {CSV_FILE} not found. Place it in the same directory.")
        return
    
    rows = load_csv_as_rows(CSV_FILE)
    print(f"\nLoaded {len(rows)} employee records from {CSV_FILE}")
    
    # Analyze context patterns
    analyze_context_patterns(rows)
    
    # Load token
    token = load_token()
    if not token:
        print("\n" + "-" * 60)
        print("NO API TOKEN FOUND")
        print("-" * 60)
        print("To run predictions with the RPT Playground API:")
        print("1. Sign up at https://rpt.cloud.sap")
        print("2. Copy your token from https://rpt.cloud.sap/docs")
        print("3. Save it to access_token.json:")
        print('   {"token": "YOUR_TOKEN_HERE"}')
        print("\nAlternatively, use the OSS version (see rpt1_oss_predict.py)")
        return
    
    # Build payload and predict
    payload = build_payload(rows, TARGET_COLUMN)
    
    try:
        result = call_rpt1_api(payload, token)
        display_predictions(result, rows)
        
        # Save raw response
        with open("rpt1_predictions.json", "w") as f:
            json.dump(result, f, indent=2)
        print(f"\nRaw predictions saved to rpt1_predictions.json")
        
    except requests.exceptions.HTTPError as e:
        print(f"\nAPI Error: {e}")
        print("Check your token and ensure the playground is accessible.")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"\nError: {e}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

Create access_token.json in the same folder with your RPT-1 API key:

{
  "token": "YOUR_RPT-API_KEY"
}

 

The Results

I ran the CSV through the RPT Playground API. Here’s exactly what came back:

EMP ID

Profile

Prediction

Confidence

Why It Makes Sense

1051

Finance • Staff • Permanent

Yes

95%

Age 25–30, Tenure 0–3 yr, Talent Program, Low premium

1052

IT • Staff • Permanent

No

98%

Age 30–40, Tenure 10–20 yr, Bonus received, Strong salary

1053

Sales • Staff • Fixed-Term

Yes

99%

Age 18–25, Tenure 0–3 yr, Fixed-term, Lowest salary

1054

HR • Manager • Permanent

No

99%

Age 40–50, Tenure 10–20 yr, Manager rank, High comp

1055

Ops • Staff • Permanent

Yes

94%

Age 25–30, Talent Prog, Retention incentive, Lower salary

 

Accuracy

Avg Confidence

Inference Time

Context Rows

5/5 (100%)

97%

399 ms

50 labeled rows

 

What Stood Out

The easy calls were easy. Employee 1053 (young, short-tenure, fixed-term, lowest salary) and Employee 1054 (senior manager, long tenure, high pay) both got 99% confidence. These are the extremes, exactly where the research paper’s Random Forest also showed the clearest separation.

Employee 1055 was the interesting one. This person has moderate tenure (3–10 years), which in the training data strongly correlates with staying. But RPT-1 correctly flagged them as at-risk at 94% confidence. Why? The combination of a retention incentive, talent program membership, and lower compensation. Remember what the research found: retention incentives are a signal that someone is already a flight risk, not a shield against it. RPT-1 picked up that interaction from just 50 context rows. That’s impressive.

399 milliseconds. The research paper required ROSE resampling, 10-fold cross-validation, hyperparameter tuning across four algorithms, and SHAP post-processing. RPT-1 got directionally equivalent predictions with a single API call on raw CSV data. For an HR team that wants a quick risk screening before committing to a full ML build, that’s a big deal.

The honest caveat: This is a 50-row synthetic dataset with intentionally clear patterns. Production data with 5,767 employees and a 5.4% attrition rate would be a harder test. These results show RPT-1 can read tabular patterns well. A head-to-head on real production data is the next step.

 

What RPT-1 Is Good At Here

  • No ML pipeline required. The research team spent serious effort on resampling, cross-validation, and algorithm selection. RPT-1 skips all of that.
  • Handles mixed data out of the box. Categorical and numerical fields go in as-is. No encoding needed.
  • Tolerates missing data. The research had to drop 5 variables due to gaps. RPT-1 handles missing values by design.
  • Easy to iterate. Want to add engagement survey scores? Just add a column. No pipeline rebuild.

 

What RPT-1 Can’t Do (Yet)

  • No explainability. RPT-1 gives you a prediction and a confidence score, but not why. For HR decisions, that matters a lot.
  • Class imbalance is your problem. With a 5.4% attrition rate, your context rows need careful curation.
  • No time-series awareness. RPT-1 works on static snapshots. It can’t predict when someone will leave.
  • Playground has limits. 2,073 rows max. For production scale, deploy via SAP AI Core.

 

So What Should You Actually Do With This?

RPT-1 makes it trivially easy to generate attrition predictions from SuccessFactors data. But predictions without understanding can lead to bad interventions. (Remember the retention incentive example: a naïve reading suggests incentives cause attrition.)

My recommendation:

  1. Use RPT-1 as a rapid screening tool. Upload your SF extract, get a risk score, identify who needs a closer look.
  2. Build a traditional ML model in parallel for explainability. Run the same features through Random Forest with SHAP to understand the why.
  3. Loop in HR. Especially for counterintuitive patterns. Data literacy plus domain knowledge turns a prediction into an action.
  4. Don’t treat aggregate results as universal. Attrition drivers for one job family can differ completely from the company-wide pattern.

 

References

  • Manafi Varkiani, S., Pattarin, F., Fabbri, T., & Fantoni, G. (2025). Predicting employee attrition and explaining its determinants. Expert Systems With Applications, 272, 126575.
  • Lundberg, S. M., & Lee, S. I. (2017). A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions. NeurIPS 2017.
  • SAP-RPT-1: sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence/sap-rpt.html
  • SAP-RPT-1-OSS: github.com/SAP-samples/sap-rpt-1-oss
  • RPT Playground: rpt.cloud.sap
  • Reference Blog — Carrier Prediction in SAP TM: SAP Community (Mar 2026)

Happy experimenting!

Built with synthetic data mirroring the variable structure and patterns from Manafi Varkiani et al. (2025). No real employee data was used. Results are illustrative.

entry.title: Employee Central Core Value Drivers – Your Self-Guided Journey to Unlock Hidden Potential
entry.content:
In today's rapidly evolving business landscape, HR teams are facing unprecedented demands to deliver strategic value while managing complex operations. That's why we're excited to introduce our new self-guided video journey that showcases some of the most impactful capabilities within SuccessFactors Employee Central.  Features specifically selected for their ability to deliver measurable business value with minimal implementation complexity.

SuccessFactors Employee Central focuses on two interconnected strategic areas that drive sustainable business value: Empowering Employees and Managers AND Scaling up HR Efficiency. 

Our curated set of 8 features support these strategic goals by transforming employee and manager experiences, providing intelligent assistance, HR automation, mass change capabilities and more.  Each feature in our self-guided video tour includes:

  • The Strategic Value: Clear explanation of why this capability matters for your business success
  • Quick Demo: See the feature in action with practical use cases
  • Configuration tips: Guidance for activating and optimizing each capability

This isn't a deep-dive technical manual.  It’s a guided discovery experience designed to help you identify which core value drivers align with your priorities and can be implemented immediately.

Why This Matters

True HR transformation extends far beyond time savings!  It's about repositioning HR as a strategic business partner. Organizations implementing these capabilities typically experience reduced administrative overhead, enhanced data quality, and improved employee satisfaction while enabling HR teams to focus on talent strategy and business-critical initiatives.

Ready to Transform Your HR Operations?

Watch SAP Success Factors Employee Central Core Value Drivers and begin unlocking the transformational potential already within your SuccessFactors Employee Central investment. Our interactive journey lets you explore high-value features at your own pace, with actionable insights you can apply immediately.  We invite you to discover them, implement them, and share your success stories with the broader HR community!

entry.title: Early Adopter Care - Central, Unified View of HR Documents in Latest People Profile for all Roles
entry.content:
As of today, viewing and accessing  documents in SuccessFactors isn't unified across the roles. IT admins have a comprehensive view through Manage Documents, but the permission model is basic and not designed for everyday business users. Meanwhile, employees, managers, and HR professionals must navigate multiple screens and modules to track down a single attachment or customers need to rely on partner solutions that provide a unified view of all documents stored for a user.

The feedback we've heard is clear: customers want a lightweight, central, unified place to go to see documents inside SuccessFactors—one that's secure, compliant, and easy for all stakeholders to use.

We’re now happy to launch an Early Adopter Care program to pilot such a central, role-based attachments experience natively inside Latest People Profile. 

Our Vision: One Profile, All Documents, Every Role

With this pilot, Latest People Profile becomes the single access point for viewing HR documents related to employees and new hires—across all user roles.

The new experience will provide role-based access that respects existing system permissions and GDPR compliance, unified visibility for employees, managers, HR, and admins, and business-independent document actions, such as view and download. 

The new feature isn't about replacing advanced document management platforms—it's about giving users a simple, central place to start when they need to find or share HR documents.

What Early Adopter Care Participants Will Get And Who Should Join

By joining the pilot, you'll be among the first to experience the unified document view inside Latest People Profile. You'll work closely with our product management and development teams to shape the feature with your expertise, and benefit from ongoing guidance during the pilot, and prioritized bug fixes.

This program is ideal for organizations that want a simple, centrally accessible document view in SuccessFactors without the overhead of a full document management solution. We're looking for companies open to piloting with a small, cross-functional user group—ideally including HR, managers, employees, and IT—to validate access rules and user experience.

How to Participate

If you are interested in this time-boxed engagement as an early adopter care customer, please see the registration page with prerequisites for joining the EAC program here: https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#/campaign/4197 
On this page you will also find the link to register for the EAC program.

Looking forward to your registration 👍

Best Regards, 

Sylvia Strangfeld,

Product Management - SAP SuccessFactors Technology Platform

entry.title: AURA Joule HR Agent for SAP SuccessFactors HCM
entry.content:
Introduction

From many SAP SuccessFactors implementations, it is clear that accessing HR data often requires navigating multiple modules, generating reports, or depending on technical teams. Even simple requests like retrieving employee or organizational details can take more time than expected.

This can impact productivity, especially for business users who need quick and reliable access to information.

SAP has introduced Joule to bring conversational capabilities into business applications. It provides a set of standard HR use cases that cover common scenarios. However, in real projects, there are always additional requirements that go beyond these standard capabilities.

To address this, we built AURA, a Joule-powered HR agent on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) using Joule Studio. The goal is to enable users to interact with SAP SuccessFactors using natural language and retrieve real-time HR data in a simpler and faster way.

Solution Overview

The AURA Joule HR Agent is designed to simplify HR data access by combining SAP’s standard capabilities with custom-built enhancements.

SAP delivers several standard Joule use cases for SAP SuccessFactors, which cover common HR scenarios and provide a solid starting point. However, during implementation, we identified gaps where business-specific or complex use cases were not fully addressed.

To bridge this gap, we built custom Joule use cases and skills tailored to organizational needs.

The solution includes:

  • A combination of SAP-delivered standard Joule use cases and custom-built use cases
  • More than 102 HR use cases across Employee Central and related modules
  • A conversational interface for natural language interaction
  • Real-time data retrieval using SAP SuccessFactors OData APIs
  • Dynamic execution of Joule Skills based on user queries
  • Unified access across multiple SuccessFactors modules

This approach was implemented in a real project scenario, where standard Joule capabilities alone were not sufficient to cover all business requirements. It combines SAP standard capabilities with custom extensions, allowing the solution to scale and adapt to real business needs.

 

Figure 1: Overview of Joule Artifacts in AURA HR Agent

 

Figure 2: Detailed View of Custom Joule Skills for HR Use Cases

 

Figure 3: Execution-Ready Joule Skills in AURA HR Solution

 

Figure 4: Custom Joule Skills for Job and Position Management

 

Figure 5: Joule Skills for Employee Data and Organizational Insights

 

Key Features

1. Scalable and Extensible Framework

  • The solution Enables continuous enhancement of HR capabilities
  • New use cases and Joule Skills can be added easily without redesigning the system.

 

Figure 6: Extensible Framework with Configurable Joule Skills

 

2. Natural Language-Based Interaction

  1. Users can simply type queries like:
  • “Show my job details”
  • “Get employee count by department”
  • No technical knowledge or navigation is required.

 

Figure 7: Natural Language Query for Location-Based Employee Data

 

3. Integration with SAP SuccessFactors Modules

AURA connects seamlessly with:

  • Employee Central
  • Foundation Data
  • Organizational Management of SAP SuccessFactors 

This ensures a unified HR data experience.

 

Figure 8: Retrieval of Employee Promotion Details Using Conversational Interface

 

4. Real-Time Data Retrieval

All responses are powered by live data fetched securely using SAP SuccessFactors OData APIs, ensuring accuracy and reliability.

 

Figure 9: Real-Time HR Data Retrieval Using SAP SuccessFactors APIs

 

5. SAP Fiori-Based Conversational UI

  • Provides a clean and intuitive user interface for interaction
  • Enhances user experience with seamless SAP integration

 

Figure 10: SAP Fiori-Based Conversational Interface for HR Queries

 

6. Dynamic Joule Skill Execution

Based on the user query, the system dynamically selects and executes the appropriate Joule Skill—automating the entire process of data retrieval and processing.

 

Figure 11: Creating a New Position Using Joule Conversational Interface

 

Benefits

1. Reduced Manual Effort

Automates HR data access and eliminates the need for manual report generation.

 

Figure 12:  Automated HR Data Retrieval Reducing Manual Effort

 

2. Improved User Productivity

Users can retrieve information instantly without switching between systems.

 

Figure 13: Instant Access to Employee Information Improving User Productivity

 

3. Faster Decision-Making

Real-time insights enable HR teams to make quick and informed decisions.

 

Figure 14: Retrieval of Active Pay Grades Using Joule Conversational Interface

 

4. Enhanced User Experience

A conversational interface simplifies system interaction, especially for non-technical users.

 

Figure 15: Conversational Interface Enhancing User Experience

 

5. Role-Based Access Control

Ensures users only access data relevant to their roles, maintaining security and compliance.

 

Figure 16: Retrieval of Active Pay Ranges Using Joule Conversational Interface

 

6. Secure and Reliable Data Access

  • Secure authentication via SAP Identity Services
  • API-based integration with proper authorization
  • Enterprise-grade security and compliance

 

Figure 17: Retrieval of Active Cost Centers Using Joule Conversational Interface

 

Conclusion

The AURA Joule HR Agent simplifies how users interact with SAP SuccessFactors by enabling conversational and real-time access to HR data.

While SAP provides standard Joule capabilities, extending them with custom use cases helps address real project requirements more effectively.

This approach improves productivity, reduces manual effort, and supports faster decision-making. It also demonstrates how Joule can be adapted to meet business-specific needs in practical implementations.

entry.title: Managing Multiple Employments in SAP SuccessFactors Learning: Person-Centric Experience
entry.content:
When Learning is employment based, each new employment for an existing employee means a new user is created in Learning, training can be assigned. Administrators then need to merge the records to maintain a unique profile per employee with all the training assignments and history.  We have already enhanced the user experience so that employees can view all assignments, pending approvals and surveys in a single view, however they may be requested to switch employment to take certain actions (like launching a course).

When Learning becomes Person Based, each new employment created in Employee Central will generate a new employment record in Learning aggregated against the same user profile. Removing the burden of continuously merging internal employee's users, ensuring continuity in learning throughout their employments.

An employee will access Learning to view AND act on ALL their assignments, pending approvals and training completion for all their employments without the need to sign-in with different accounts.

Adopting the Person Centric Experience in Learning entails:

  1. Preparing your user data (select a single user profile per employees, merge other records to the main user)
  2. Set-up the new Person Connector (replacing the SF User connector)
  3. Migrate the tenant

Please note the following prerequisites:

  1. You must have more than one preview environment
    This is because to enable Person Based Learning, you need to migrate the instance and this will change how the user data is handled in the application. Therefore this system will behave differently than you production instance. It is important to keep a non-production instance that is NOT person based to match your production system where you can carry out tests and change management.
  2. Employee Central enabled
  3. Your Platform application is migrated to IAS enabled
  4. The PLT–LMS integration of the preview instance is sealed 

If you are interested and meet the prerequisites, you can submit a support ticket after the 1H2026 preview release (please share your system details and evidence  that the above conditions are met) so we that can enable a feature toggle in your instance to enable the migration feature.

To understand what the migration entails and how the application behaves when it is person based, please read the materials available here , the content provided are as follow:

  • Transition Guide = covers the overall migration process and end user experience post migration.
  • Migration Guide = covers details of the migration process
  • Person Connector Guide = covers implementation details for the new connector
  • Person Connector Integration Center: File Definition = Integration Center icd template
  • Person Connector Integration Center: File Specification = Integration Center field mapping details
  • Person Connector Fields and Properties = Person connector mapping and properties

entry.title: Import of Attachments in HRIS Elements
entry.content:
Introduction :

We had to Bulk Upload attachments to the Contract Extension Proof  (Attachment Field) Job Information. I am outlining the steps involved in importing attachments and the challenges faced during the import , as the KBA published by SAP is not very detailed.

The Process :

Step 1 :

The Process starts with downloading the import template from

Import Employee Data -> Download Template -> Job Information (In this case ) , You can choose the HRIS Element of your Choice

Make sure the attachment field is added to the Template

Step 2 : Preparing the Import . properties File

This is the crucial part of the import process and this is where most of the consultants overlook the extension and will be thrown with the below error during Import


Invalid or Missing property: {​​0}​​ in import.properties

Follow the Below Steps to prepare the import.properties file

 

  1. Create a New Folder (SAP in my Case)
  2. Create a New Notepad File
  3. Copy paste the below into the Notepad File importFileName=YOUR_IMPORT_TEMPLATE_NAME.csv

     

  4. Click Save as


    Make sure to set the Save as Type to All Files. The File should like this after the save


    Step 3 : Preparing the Import Template

    Open the Import Template 

    Make sure the name of the file is exactly the same suffixed with Extension.

    The Folder should look like this before Zipping

    Zip the File and the Zipped file should look like this 


Step 4 : Validate and Import the File

Conclusion :

Key Takeaways

  • The import.properties file must not have .txt extension
  • File names must match exactly (case-sensitive recommended)
  • All files must be placed in the same folder before zipping
  • Zip the files together, not individually
  • Always run validation before import

entry.title: SuccessFactors Business Rules: Now it's time for a deep dive in the format() function!
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I want to share with you some tips around the format function. It’s one of the most important functions used in Business Rules, as it allows you to combine different elements into meaningful text. For example, you can use it to merge a country code (e.g., DE), a business unit name (e.g., SMB), and a number (e.g., 111) into a single value like DESMB111.

The format function is also useful for tasks such as concatenation, adding leading zeros to codes, formatting dates, and more. For a deeper dive, check out the Implementing Business Rules in SAP SuccessFactors document in the Help Portal.

Here are some examples: 

Simple Format: Let’s create a basic template. In this example, we are generating a position code:

  • % → start of the formatter
  • d → conversion digit

Upper Letter format [Strings: %s vs %S]: Some customers would like to store the first and last name information in uppercase.

As you can see above, the function has the following behavior:

  • %s → prints the string as-is
  • %S → converts the entire string to uppercase

Different Arguments [%N$] : This function can also be used to concatenate information from different fields. I personally prefer using it because it makes it easy to include elements like spaces between words.

  • %1$s → first argument (First Name)
  • %2$s → second argument (Last Name)

Adding leading zeros: In some cases, it’s necessary to add leading zeros to an external code. For example, a position code might need to be 8 digits long and generated sequentially.

  • 08 → represents the max number of digits that should be considered

Substring: This function can also be used to extract a substring, such as when defaulting the initials.

  • .1 → represents the number of chars that will be considered from the text

Rounding: The format function can also be used to display a decimal value with a specific number of decimal places.

  • .2 → represents the number of decimal places that should be considered from the calculation
  • f → floating type

Date/Time formatting: You can also use the function to format information based on date and time.

In terms of date/time formats here is a small list of the most used formatters:

FormatterDescriptionExample

%tY

Year (4 digits)

2026

%ty

Year (2 digits)

26

%tm

Month (01–12)

04

%td

Day (01–31)

06

%tB

Full month name

April

%tb

Short month name

Apr

%tA

Full weekday

Monday

%ta

Short weekday

Mon

%tH

Hour (00–23)

14

%tI

Hour (01–12)

02

%tM

Minute

35

%tS

Seconds

20

%tp

am/pm

pm

%tF

ISO date

2026-04-06

%tD

US date

04/06/26

%tc

Full date/time

Mon Apr 06 14:35:20 UTC 2026

%tr

12-hour time

02:35:20 PM

%tT

24-hour time

14:35:20

I hope this information has been helpful and encourages you to explore our standard functions to streamline your own implementation projects! As always, please discuss with your customers the pros and cons of adopting such a function in your projects.
